items.question: Who uses L1 and L2 regularization to combat overfitting?
items.answer: ML engineers and data scientists training linear models, logistic regression, and neural networks use L1/L2 penalties when validation error exceeds training error or when models have many parameters relative to data.

items.question: What is L1/L2 regularization?
items.answer: It is adding a weight penalty Ω(w) to the training loss: L_total = L_data + λ Ω(w). L2 uses Σ wⱼ² (shrinkage); L1 uses Σ |wⱼ| (can zero weights for sparsity).

items.question: When should you apply L1 vs L2 regularization?
items.answer: Apply L2 when you want smooth shrinkage and stable handling of correlated features; apply L1 when you need sparse models with many coefficients driven to zero. Tune λ when train ≪ val error signals overfitting.

items.question: Where does the penalty appear in the objective?
items.answer: The penalty λ Ω(θ) is added to the data loss L_data(θ)—for example MSE or cross-entropy—so the optimizer balances fit quality against weight magnitude during training.

items.question: Why does regularization reduce overfitting?
items.answer: Large weights let the model amplify noise in inputs; penalizing magnitude restricts effective capacity, lowering prediction variance across training samples at the cost of some bias when λ is large.

items.question: How do you choose the regularization strength λ?
items.answer: Search a log-spaced range of λ values and pick the one with lowest cross-validated error (or within one standard error of the minimum). Never select λ by training loss alone.

items.question: L2 vs L1 regularization — shrinkage vs sparsity?
items.answer: L2 penalizes squared weights and shrinks all coefficients toward zero but rarely zeros them—good for correlated features. L1 penalizes absolute weights and often sets some to exactly zero—good for sparse feature selection but may drop one of a correlated group.

items.question: Regularization vs collecting more training data?
items.answer: More data reduces variance without intentionally increasing bias and is often the best fix when feasible. Regularization is a capacity constraint when data is limited or models are flexible—it trades bias for variance via the λ knob.

items.question: Small λ vs large λ — bias and variance?
items.answer: Small λ is closer to unregularized fitting: lower bias, higher variance risk. Large λ strongly shrinks weights: lower variance but higher bias, potentially underfitting when both train and validation error stay high.

items.question: Cause: flexible model with large unconstrained weights on noisy training data. Effect?
items.answer: Training error falls while validation error stays high—overfitting. Predictions vary across retrained models because weights chase sample noise (high variance).

items.question: Cause: adding moderate L2 penalty with λ tuned on validation error. Effect?
items.answer: Weight magnitudes shrink, the train–validation gap narrows, and out-of-sample error often improves until λ becomes so large that both train and validation error rise (underfitting).
